text: My name is Dr. Vishal Soni and I will be demonstrating a case of robotic tap meshplasty for a case of bilateral inguinal hernia.
The surgeon places ports and begins a peritoneal incision approximately two centimeters from the horizontal line at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).
The surgeon enters the extraperitoneal or preperitoneal space, advancing through the initial incision made near the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).
The surgeon encounters loose areolar tissue and uses cold scissors along with short bursts of energy to create a wide dissection plane, ensuring no major vessels are visible within the plane.
The dissection is carried out in a very systematic manner.
The way the zones are described, the zone one, zone two and zone three, the lateral, the medial and the central zones.
In robotic surgery, the dissection is performed horizontally, contrasting with the vertical approach typically seen in laparoscopic procedures.

The surgeon enters the medial compartment of zone two, where the rectal branch of the inferior epigastric artery is first encountered, indicating correct dissection progress.

The surgeon continues dissection in the lateral pelvic wall region, aiming to reach three centimeters below the pubic arch to establish an adequate plane. The bony landmark known as the lighthouse will soon be visible, confirming the correct dissection path.
